name: Baku
description: The Baku is a supernatural being in Japanese folklore, a chimera-like creature known for its ability to devour nightmares. Its appearance is often described as a composite of various animals. Depictions vary, but a common representation includes the head of an elephant, the body of a bear, the legs of a tiger, and the tail of an ox. Some accounts also include the eyes of a rhinoceros. The Baku is considered a benevolent spirit, invoked to protect people from bad dreams. Legend states that if a person is having a nightmare, they can chant "Baku-san, come eat my dream!" three times, and the Baku will appear and devour the nightmare, leaving the person to sleep peacefully. However, it is also said that if the Baku remains hungry after eating all the nightmares, it may consume the dreamer's hopes and desires, leaving them an empty shell. Therefore, invoking the Baku should be done with caution.
subtitle: The Dream Eater
appearance: A chimera with the head of an elephant, the body of a bear, the legs of a tiger, and the tail of an ox. Some depictions include rhinoceros eyes. Its fur is often described as shaggy and multi-colored.
habitat: The realm of dreams; Shrines and temples (sometimes depicted on pillows and tapestries).
behavior: Generally benevolent, appearing only when invoked to consume nightmares. Can be unpredictable if left hungry, potentially consuming the dreamer's spirit.
weaknesses: Starvation (leading to consuming the dreamer's spirit)
Certain sacred objects (can repel it if misused)

keyMyths.title: The Origin of the Baku
keyMyths.description: While the exact origins are debated, many believe the Baku originated in Chinese folklore and was later adopted into Japanese beliefs. It was initially depicted as a creature that warded off evil spirits and pestilence, eventually evolving into the dream-eating entity it is known as today.

keyMyths.title: Invoking the Baku
keyMyths.description: The most common myth involves individuals chanting "Baku-san, come eat my dream!" when experiencing a nightmare. This is believed to summon the Baku to consume the disturbing dream, providing the dreamer with peaceful sleep. However, repeating the chant excessively is discouraged.

keyMyths.title: The Baku's Hunger
keyMyths.description: A cautionary tale warns of the dangers of over-reliance on the Baku. If the Baku is not satisfied with the nightmares it consumes, it may turn its attention to the dreamer's hopes, dreams, and even their life force, leaving them empty and devoid of ambition. This reinforces the idea of moderation.

keyMyths.title: Baku and the Emperor
keyMyths.description: Some legends tell of emperors commissioning images of the Baku to be placed on their pillows and tapestries, believing it would protect them from nightmares and evil influences. This highlights the Baku's association with protection and good fortune.
keyMyths.source: Imperial Records of the Edo Period

sources.author: Hearn, Lafcadio
sources.work: Kwaidan: Stories and Studies of Strange Things
sources.description: Provides insights into Japanese folklore and supernatural creatures, including brief mentions and cultural context of the Baku.
sources.text: Provides insights into Japanese folklore and supernatural creatures, including brief mentions and cultural context of the Baku.
